timeline.text:
An engineer ran GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra on a branch with 38 files. Seven minutes later, he did not get a patch and he got 4 subagents arguing over who could merge the code.

38 files. 4 agents. 12 failing tests. 1 empty verify.md.

One agent edited the migration, another rewrote the mock, and a third suggested deleting the guard that stopped duplicate payments. 

The tests were not green yet, but the confidence was glowing.

So he opened verify.md and wrote 3 rules. 

Every claim needed file:line, a real test, and a separate verifier that had not seen the first agent’s reasoning.

That was the uncomfortable part. 

The wider the agent system gets, the faster it can produce beautiful garbage when nothing stands between loop and main.

The next race is not for the smartest agent. It is for the team that puts a verifier in front of merge.

timeline.text:
Models like GPT 5.6 or Fable 5 are accelerating the commodification of software. For instance, I gave Fable a screenshot of the Stripe, OpenAI, and Anthropic websites with a simple prompt: “Create a pixel-perfect reproduction of this website.”

It took only seconds and a simple prompt to duplicate the design. This is exciting because there are so many poorly designed websites, but now they are just a prompt away from achieving world-class design!!

timeline.text:
GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra, and Luna won't improve your results. WHY?!

In practice, things work differently.

The smarter the models become, the more important it becomes to formulate the right problem.

➤ A good prompt helps:
- get better answers
- spend fewer tokens
- reduce the number of iterations
- use the same template in different models
- save time and money.

This is especially important now that more and more AI services are moving to pay-per-use models.

Every extra request, every clarification, and every rework means extra tokens and additional costs.

➤ Therefore, with the release of GPT-5.6, Sol, and Terra, it's worth learning not only how to use AI.

You need to learn how to explain to the AI ​​exactly what you want to achieve.

How? Read the article.

ELON JUST DECLARED WAR ON ANTHROPIC

Grok 4.5 is live — and Musk’s pitch is literally “Opus-class, but faster and cheaper.”

What’s actually happening:

→ $2/$6 per million tokens (Opus 4.8 costs $5/$25)
→ First model trained WITH Cursor — the $60B acquisition at work
→ Built on the same compute Elon leases to Anthropic and Google
→ 500K context, live in Cursor on all plans

The plot twist: Musk’s own internal assessment says “roughly comparable to Opus 4.7.”

Not 4.8. Not Fable 5.

He’s not winning on intelligence. He’s winning on price.

And GPT-5.6 goes wide Thursday. Three frontier releases in one week.

Models are becoming commodities. Your workflow is the moat.

I broke down how to turn this chaos into an advantage 👇

timeline.text:
I made Fable 5 the architect and GPT 5.6 Soul the builder — and stopped paying premium prices for grunt work

Soul 5.6 just beat Mythos and Fable on Terminal Bench. But nobody's asking the right question: why choose between them when you can chain them? I built a skill that does exactly that — Fable plans, Soul executes.

It runs in four steps:

– Step 1: An interview about the feature you're building powered by the "grill me" skill from Matt Pocock. Think plan mode on steroids

– Step 2: Adversarial planning. Fable writes the plan, then hands it to Codex. They argue back and forth for 5 iterations until they reach consensus — two sets of eyes on every idea

– Step 3: The finalized plan goes to Soul 5.6 for execution — the cheaper model does the heavy lifting

– Step 4: Fable reviews the output at the end. Quality gate stays premium

Result: advisor mode, ramped up. Instead of burning Opus-level pricing on every line of code, the expensive model only plans and reviews — the parts where it's actually irreplaceable. Execution goes to the model that's better AND cheaper at it.

The full skill drops tomorrow — plus the one prompt in the adversarial step that decides whether the whole chain works or collapses.

user_info.name: Psalter
user_info.description: For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also

timeline.text:
OpenAI did not just preview a stronger model.

It previewed a coordination problem.

GPT-5.6 Sol has ultra mode now.

That means subagents.

More width.
More parallel work.
More long-horizon execution.

But the question nobody wants to ask is simple:

who checks the merge?

The new stack is not:

- Bigger model
- More agents
- Longer loops
- More tools
- More autonomy

The new stack is:

- Plan
- Spawn
- Execute
- Verify
- Merge

Loop engineering moves you from writing prompts to designing stop conditions.

GPT-5.6 answers how to go wider. Loops answer how to keep work moving. The verifier layer decides whether any of it deserves to ship.

user_info.name: Chetaslua
user_info.description: AI Insider / Reporter featured in BGR • HackerNews • GIGAZINE • 36Kr | AI Prompting and Testing | Vibe Benchmark and Vibe Marketing 🫆

timeline.text:
GPT-5.6 vs GPT-5.5 on my custom spaceship prompt.

I gave both models the exact same custom prompt. This is also the same prompt I previously gave to Fable 5.

For context, GPT-5.6 Pro worked for 87 minutes, while GPT-5.5 Extra High worked for 34 minutes and 42 seconds.

As I’ve said before, based on great authority GPT-5.6 will be an incremental/soldi improvement over GPT-5.5, not a “Fable killer.” My rough expectation has been that it would trade blows with Fable 5 on some benchmarks, maybe win around half depending on the category, but not clearly surpass it overall. And again fable five will have bigger model smell, but this was expected.

After testing this coding output, that view feels pretty accurate.

GPT-5.6 is clearly better than GPT-5.5 in several visual areas. The lighting, shading, chairs, object details, and exterior of the spaceship looked noticeably stronger. The scene was also easier to test.

I do want to give GPT-5.5 credit though. It built out the rooms much much better and the planets looked better than GPT-5.6’s. It was also interesting that both GPT-5.5 and GPT-5.6 produced better-looking planets than Fable 5 in this specific test.

The downside with GPT-5.5 was stability. The game was much glitchier and harder to test compared to GPT-5.6.

But when it comes to the core of the demo, which is the spaceship itself, Fable 5 still beat both models pretty comfortably. GPT-5.6 is impressive, but from this test, it looks exactly like what I expected which was a meaningful incremental improvement over GPT-5.5, at least for indie game demos, but not something that replaces Fable 5.

In collaboration with @chetaslua

timeline.text:
🔥 Grok 4.5 est incroyable !

Je l'ai comparé à GPT 5.6 Pro et Fable 5, qui comptent parmi les meilleurs modèles, et pourtant, il a réussi à m'impressionner.

Il lui a fallu environ 8 minutes pour générer ce résultat, avec seulement 900 lignes de code.

Tout fonctionne du premier coup, sans aucune erreur (j'ai dû corriger quelques problèmes avec GPT 5.6 et Fable 5). Les textures sont réussies, sans éclairage excessif.

Il reste encore quelques défauts, comme le frigo orienté dans le mauvais sens, mais avec quelques itérations supplémentaires, le résultat pourrait être excellent !

quoted.text:
GPT 5.6 Pro m'a encore surpris... 😮

La dernière fois, je lui ai fait générer une maison entière en 3D. Cette fois-ci, je lui ai demandé de concentrer tous ses efforts sur une seule pièce.

Tous les assets ont été créés en one-shot dans un unique fichier .html, et on peut même https://t.co/TD4hHtqiku

timeline.text:
GPT 5.6 Pro me prouve encore qu'il comprend très bien la 3D.

En seulement 30 minutes, il a généré une maison complète en 3D dans un unique fichier .html, en utilisant uniquement WebGL2.

La structure est cohérente, les volumes sont crédibles et l'ensemble est directement navigable dans le navigateur. Il reste quelques incohérences, notamment sur certaines fenêtres, mais la progression est impressionnante.

À ce rythme, ce type de défauts risque de disparaître bien plus vite qu'on ne l'imagine 👀
